title: NASA Administrator: Artemis II Is 'Just the Beginning' — Permanent Moon Base and Mars on Horizon
detail: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman, appearing on ABC News' Good Morning America on April 13, declared that Artemis II is only the opening act of a much larger ambition. 'We got the mandate, the resources, we have a successful mission — Artemis II — under our belt. We are just getting going,' Isaacman said. He framed the mission's post-splashdown momentum as a signal of long-term program intent: the agency's near-term roadmap calls for Artemis IV to land astronauts near the lunar South Pole in 2028, with Trump's December 2025 executive order mandating initial elements of a permanent lunar outpost by 2030. Isaacman emphasized that sustained human presence on the Moon is a prerequisite — both technologically and politically — for eventual crewed Mars missions. The statement reinforces the program's shift away from the cancelled Lunar Gateway architecture toward a direct surface-base approach announced at the March 24 'Ignition' event. Congressional allies echoed the framing, with multiple members citing Artemis II's success as evidence that the accelerated timeline is achievable. The Orion capsule is currently en route to Kennedy Space Center for its 30-day heat shield inspection, results of which will directly inform Artemis IV's crewed landing readiness.

sources.name: ABC News: NASA administrator says Artemis II is 1st step toward moon base, Mars missions (Apr 13, 2026)

media.caption: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman on Good Morning America: Artemis II is the first step toward a permanent lunar base and eventual Mars missions

title: NASA Formally Shifts Programmatic Focus to Artemis III — Crewed Lunar Landing Milestones Outlined
detail: Following the successful Artemis II crewed lunar flyby, NASA has formally shifted its programmatic focus to Artemis III, outlining the path toward future crewed lunar landing missions and near-term milestones. The agency is leveraging the momentum from Artemis II's completion to consolidate planning for the next phase of the lunar exploration program. Key milestones include continued development of the Human Landing System, SLS Block 1B configuration assessment, and coordination with international partners for the broader Artemis architecture. The programmatic pivot underscores NASA's intent to maintain operational tempo and translate mission success into sustained lunar surface access.

sources.name: Google News: NASA shifts focus to Artemis III following Artemis II success (Apr 13, 2026)
